text:
Admission Date:  1987-04-04       Discharge Date:  1991-04-02

Date of Birth:   1971-02-13       Sex:  F

Service:  CARDIOT Cynthia

CHIEF COMPLAINT:   Elective coronary artery bypass graft and
aortic valve replacement.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:   This is a 76 year old female
who had a new murmur discovered in 2000.  The patient had an
echocardiogram done at Lanai Community Hospital Hospital in 1985-10-23.
This revealed an ejection fraction of approximately 50% and
severe aortic stenosis.  The patient complains of increasing
dyspnea on exertion with one flight of stairs.

The patient denies any chest pain, pressure, lightheadedness,
nausea or sweating.  The patient was referred to our Cardiac
Catheterization Laboratory at the Lanai Community Hospital Hospital.  The
catheterization was done on 2007-05-23.

The results of this are as follows:
1.  Right dominant artery; left anterior descending, tubular
75% lesion; normal right and left heart filling pressures;
normal cardiac output.
2.  Aortic valve disease with severe stenosis.  Peak gradient
across the aortic valve is 60 millimeters of Mercury with [**Initials (NamePattern4) **]
Powell of 0.85 centimeters squared.

The patient was referred to Dr. Andrews for an aortic valve
replacement and coronary artery bypass graft.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1.  Glaucoma.
2.  Hypercholesterolemia.
3.  Hypertension.
4.  Status post appendectomy.
5.  Status post bilateral cataract surgery.
6.  Status post left breast biopsy.

MEDICATIONS AT HOME:
1.  Lipitor 40 mg p.o. q. day.
2.  Hydrochlorothiazide unknown dose.
3.  Eye drops.

ALLERGIES:   No known drug allergies.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:  The patient denies any coughs, chest
pain, syncope, palpitations, bleeding disorders, diabetes
mellitus, thyroid disease, transient ischemic attack,
cerebrovascular accident, claudication and varicosities.  The
patient endorses dyspnea on exertion.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:   On examination, the patient was
afebrile; heart rate 70; blood pressure 160/81 on the left
and 155/83 on the right.  Cardiac examination was regular
rate and rhythm, S1, S2, with a holosystolic murmur which
radiates to the carotids.  Her lung examination was clear to
auscultation bilaterally.  The patient was breathing evenly
and unlabored.  Abdominal examination was soft and nontender,
nondistended, with positive bowel sounds; no
hepatosplenomegaly was appreciated.  No costovertebral angle
tenderness.  Extremities were warm and well perfused.  No
erythema, edema or varicosities were noted.  Pulse
examination:  The patient had two plus pulses bilaterally in
the carotids, radial, femoral and posterior tibial.
Neurological examination:  Cranial nerves II through XII are
grossly intact.

LABORATORY:  On admission, white blood cell count 12.6,
hematocrit 37.0, platelets 380.  PT 12.5, PTT 25.5, INR 1.0.
Sodium 141, potassium 4.1, chloride 101, bicarbonate 34, BUN
20, creatinine 1.0.  Blood glucose is 98.  Total bilirubin is
0.6, albumin 4.0, ALT 18, AST 18, alkaline phosphatase 113.

Electrocardiograms done on this date showed normal sinus
rhythm at 69 beats per minute with nonspecific lateral ST
changes; questionable left ventricular hypertrophy.

BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE:   On 06-02, the patient was
admitted for coronary artery bypass graft and aortic valve
replacement.  The patient underwent a bronchoscopy which
revealed a normal oropharynx, moderate edema within the
larynx, old clots in the trachea, slight aspiration in the
main stem bronchi.  There were also mucosal abnormalities 2
centimeters below the cords with no acute bleeding and no
obvious lesion.

The patient underwent her aortic valve replacement and
coronary artery bypass graft.  The patient had her left
internal mammary artery to her left anterior descending and a
19 millimeter pericardial valve placed.  The procedure was
performed by Dr. Anthony Guerrero and assisted by Dr. Ann
Bernard and nurse Alvin Ann Bernard.  The crossclamp
time was 77 minutes and the coronary bypass time was 115
minutes.

During the bronchoscopy, there was also an evacuation of a
tracheal clot.  The patient tolerated the procedures well and
there were no complications during the surgeries.

Following a brief stay in the Post Anesthesia Care Unit, the
patient was transferred to the CSRU on a phenylephrine and
Propofol drip.

Upon arrival in the CSRU, the patient was noted to have
labile blood pressure and was maintained on her
Neo-Synephrine.  The patient was weaned off her vent and
slowly weaned off her Neo-Synephrine.

On postoperative day number one, the patient was weaned off
SIMP to CPAP with the pressure support of 5 and 5.  She was
started on Lopressor and Lasix and had one episode of atrial
fibrillation.  The patient was weaned off of her
neo-synephrine and she was able to maintain her blood
pressure without any additional pressors.

On postoperative day number two, it was noted that the
patient had a fairly large amount of secretions and
questionable blood tinged sputum.  The patient was started on
Levofloxacin.

The patient underwent a second bronchoscopy which revealed a
moderate sized blood clot at the left and right main stem
bronchi.  Due to the overly thick secretions and large
quantities, the patient was maintained on intubation.  The
patient was extubated late on postoperative day number two.

Following extubation, the patient was given aggressive
pulmonary toilet which she secreted thick dark red blood
clots.  The patient was maintained on a face mask and was
able to maintain her O2 saturation at greater than 90% while
on 50% oxygen.

From a cardiovascular standpoint, the patient continued to be
in atrial fibrillation while on intravenous Lopressor.  The
patient was placed on Nitroglycerin for hypertension at a
drip of 0.5 micrograms per kilogram per minute.  The patient
was given an amiodarone bolus and started on an amiodarone
drip for her atrial fibrillation.

On postoperative day number three, the patient was weaned
down on her Nitroglycerin after starting a low dose Captopril
and Lopressor.  The patient continued to be weaned off her
face mask down to nasal cannula and was able to maintain her
oxygen saturations without any difficulty.  On this day, the
patient was off of her amiodarone drip and placed on
amiodarone p.o.  The patient continued to have labile blood
pressure and her Nitroglycerin had to be increased up to 1.5
micrograms per kilogram per minute, although the patient
remained in normal sinus rhythm with no ectopy noted.

By postoperative day number four, the patient was able to be
weaned off all pressors and was labelled to be in normal
sinus rhythm and maintained her O2 saturations on small
amounts of nasal cannula oxygen.  The patient was transferred
to the floor on that same day.

Once the patient reached the floor, the patient was seen by
Physical Therapy.  Physical Therapy stated that after working
with her for a couple of sessions, she would be able to be
discharged to home.

Once the patient reached the floor, the remainder of her
postoperative course was relatively uneventful.  The patient
had her pacing wires removed on postoperative day number six
and the patient was able to maintain her blood pressure and
remain in normal sinus rhythm without any difficulty.  The
patient was started on Coumadin on postoperative day number
seven in anticipation of her going home while needing
anticoagulation.

By postoperative day number eight, the patient was cleared by
Physical Therapy and her INR was in a therapeutic range.  Her
heparin was discontinued and plans were made to discharge the
patient to home.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  On discharge, the patient was afebrile
with a temperature maximum of 97.4 F.; pulse 70 in sinus
rhythm; blood pressure of 119/85; breathing 18; 100% on room
air.

Her labs on discharge were CBC with white blood cell count of
10.9, hematocrit 29, platelets 286.  Chem-7;  Sodium 139,
potassium 4.9, chloride 102, bicarbonate 32, BUN 16,
creatinine 1.2, blood glucose 19.  PT 19.9, PTT 55.8, INR
2.6.

The patient was alert and oriented times three in no apparent
distress.  Her lung examination was clear to auscultation
bilaterally breathing even and unlabored.  She was in a
regular rate and rhythm with S1 and S2 noted.  Her sternum
was stable and incision with Steri-Strips.  The incision was
clean, dry and intact.  Her abdomen was soft, nontender,
nondistended, with noticeable bowel sounds.  Her extremities
were warm and well perfused with a one plus pedal edema.

DISCHARGE DISPOSITION:   The patient was discharged to home
with Visiting Nurses Association nursing provided by 26643 Dawn Shore Apt. 226
Lamside, PR 81149-6301
Visiting Nurses Association, telephone number 428-665-2918.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS:
1.  The patient was instructed to please follow-up with Dr.
Derek in two to three weeks.
2.  The patient was instructed to follow-up with Dr. Andrews in
four weeks.
3.  The patient was also instructed to have her INR checked
in 24 hours and have the results called in to Dr. Anthony
Guerrero for further Coumadin dosing.

CONDITION ON DISCHARGE:   The patient's condition was good.
She was afebrile.  Pain was under control with oral
medications, tolerating a regular diet without difficulty and
ambulating independently.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:
1.  Status post aortic valve replacement.
2.  Status post coronary artery bypass grafting, LMA to left
anterior descending.
3.  Status post postoperative atrial fibrillation.
4.  Hypercholesterolemia.
5.  Glaucoma.
6.  Hypertension.
7.  Status post appendectomy.
8.  Status post bilateral cataract surgery.

DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS:
1.  Metoprolol 50 mg p.o. twice a day.
2.  Potassium chloride 20 mEq p.o. twice a day for two weeks.
3.  Percocet one to two tablets p.o. q. four to six hours
p.r.n. pain.
4.  Amiodarone 400 mg p.o. twice a day for three days, then
200 mg twice a day for seven days, and 200 mg q. day times
one month.
5.  Captopril 37.5 mg p.o. three times a day.
6.  Aspirin 81 mg p.o. q. day.
7.  Lasix 20 mg p.o. q. day for two weeks.
8.  Coumadin 2 mg tablets, taken as directed, to maintain
goal INR of 2.0 to 2.5.
9.  Lipitor 40 mg tablets p.o. q. day.


                            [**First Name11 (Name Pattern1) 275**] Jones, M.D.  21726

Dictated By:Jose Walker
MEDQUIST36
